---
title: "CellShard Behavior"
---

CellShard presently functions as the storage and execution-staging substrate beneath Cellerator rather than as a general analytical environment. Its operational role begins only after Cellerator has finished the shape-changing phases of ingest: filtering, metadata alignment, feature and observation selection, and the one-pass emission of an immutable canonical sparse matrix. From that point forward, CellShard assumes responsibility for the durable representation of that matrix, for the derivation of execution-facing layouts from it, and for the controlled publication of those derived artifacts to the readers and executors that consume them.

The central distinction in its behavior is the separation between canonical persistence and hot execution. The `.csh5` container is the durable source of truth. It holds the authoritative dataset header, partition and shard boundaries, payload arrays, and the runtime-service metadata needed to identify generations and ownership. The `.pack` artifacts derived from that container are not archival objects; they are execution artifacts. CellShard materializes versioned pack generations from `.csh5`, serves those generations for repeated multithreaded access, and treats them as the fast path for host fetch, delivery, and downstream staging. In practical terms, `.csh5` remains the stable canonical body of the dataset, whereas `.pack` carries the transient but operationally decisive form in which that dataset is read at runtime.

This distinction is reinforced by an owner-hosted service model that persists even when the entire workflow resides on a single machine. Canonical file access is intentionally narrow. One owner-side coordinator governs generation identity and publication state; one master reader retains responsibility for canonical `.csh5` access during hot operation; pack-preparation workers materialize or refresh execution generations; executor threads or nodes consume those published generations rather than mutating the canonical source in place. The absence of a network hop in single-machine mode does not relax the contract. It merely collapses roles that remain logically separate: canonical ownership, pack preparation, and execution still occupy distinct positions in the runtime.

In distributed operation the same contract is extended rather than replaced. The owner host retains the canonical `.csh5`, prepares the relevant pack generation, and routes pack data to remote executors. Executor nodes may retain delivered pack artifacts in node-local caches, but those caches remain operational conveniences rather than sources of truth. The authoritative dataset is not redefined by the location of a hot copy. It remains anchored to the owner-hosted canonical container, while the published pack generation defines the current readable execution epoch.

CellShard's live behavior is therefore governed by publication and cutover rather than by in-place mutation. Reads proceed against versioned pack generations. Append staging may occur concurrently, but new material does not become part of the active runtime view until a new generation has been prepared and explicitly published. Canonical payloads are append-only during hot reads; overwrite or relocation is reserved for explicit maintenance conditions. This gives the system a precise temporal structure: immutable canonical generations, separately prepared execution generations, and a service epoch that determines which prepared state is currently visible to readers.

The practical consequence is that CellShard should not be understood as a thin file wrapper around sparse payloads. It is a runtime discipline for keeping canonical storage, derived execution state, and reader visibility rigorously separated. Cellerator hands over an immutable matrix. CellShard persists it, derives execution-facing layouts from it, publishes those layouts as pack generations, and preserves a narrow ownership boundary around the canonical source while local or remote executors consume the published result.
